text.title: roadmap-design Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for roadmap-design. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Select roadmap format by uncertainty level: low uncertainty → timeline; medium → Now-Next-Later; high → outcome-themed; live execution → kanban.
rule.rationale: Timeline format with hard dates always slips and erodes trust when uncertainty is high. Now-Next-Later communicates priority without false precision and is the default for early-stage products.

rule.statement: Use Now-Next-Later as the default internal format unless you have high confidence in delivery dates AND a stakeholder audience that requires commitments.
rule.rationale: PMs who default to timeline roadmaps spend more time explaining slippage than building. Now-Next-Later shifts the conversation to priority, not dates.

rule.statement: Generate two artifacts from one source — internal (with confidence levels, dependencies) and external (with capability descriptions, no internals). Never maintain them in parallel.
rule.rationale: Parallel maintenance diverges within one cycle. One source of truth with a formatter subagent prevents inconsistency and saves the PM from double-editing.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Collect strategy inputs before defining themes: product vision, business goals for the period, user research insights, and technical constraints.
rule.rationale: Themes without strategic inputs produce feature-grouped buckets, not outcome-driven work areas.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Define 3–5 themes per period; each theme must name the objective it drives and provide 2–3 example initiatives.
rule.rationale: More than 5 themes is aspirational for solo/small teams and dilutes focus. Each theme without an objective link cannot be defended under prioritization pressure.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Tag each initiative with a confidence level (high/medium/low) and treat "high" as a commitment only when scoped and spec'd.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders interpret "high confidence" as a promise. Calibrate against past quarter delivery to set a realistic confidence mix.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Always include a "Not Doing" section with explicit exclusions and reasons.
rule.rationale: The cheapest scope-creep prevention. A roadmap without "Not Doing" is an invitation to add more.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Review monthly, rewrite quarterly. Add a "last updated" date prominently. Cap "Later" at 8 items; cull once per quarter.
rule.rationale: Stale roadmaps lose trust faster than missing ones. "Later" becomes a dumping ground without a cull policy.
